The most notable increase in sector allocation during the fourth quarter (and for the calendar year) was an increase in mortgage-backed securities (MBS). Prior to 2023, the Fund had not had a meaningful investment in MBS. Since that time, we have steadily increased our allocation to over 30%. Our focus has been and remains higher coupon agency MBS, with coupons of 4.50%-6.50%. Agency MBS is an area within benchmark categories that has rarely looked more attractive relative to investment grade corporate bonds. Low investment grade spreads can make a lot of alternatives look good but the high-quality nature of agency MBS and what we believe is a better return profile relative to investment grade corporate bonds remain a positive investment theme for our investment portfolios.

In addition to MBS, we added Treasury securities, principally in 10–20-year maturities. We also added a wide variety of securitized investments, including investments within Auto ABS, CLOs, CMBS, and whole business.

In terms of overall portfolio metrics, the Fund’s average effective maturity increased to 9.5 years as of December 31, 2024, from 9.0 years as of September 30, 2024, while our average effective duration increased to 5.8 years from 5.5 years over the same period, compared to the Agg’s average effective duration of 6.0 years on December 31. These measures provide a guide to the Fund’s interest rate sensitivity. A higher average effective duration increases the Fund’s price sensitivity to changes in interest rates (either up or down).

As of December 31, our high-yield exposure as a percentage of net assets stayed at 3.9%, similarly to September 30, 2024. The Fund can invest up to 25% of net assets in high yield, therefore we have ample capacity to take advantage of valuation discrepancies/ opportunities in the high yield area.

The Fund’s yield to worst (YTW) remains higher than the Bloomberg Barclays U.S. Aggregate index, while the Fund’s interest rate risk (duration) continues to be lower. Constructing a portfolio that produces “carry” – income achieved from regular coupon payments – (via the YTW measurement) and lower interest rate sensitivity (via lower duration) is a feature of our flexible mandate and ability to invest in other higher quality securities that reside outside of the benchmark. The Fund’s 30-day SEC yield was 5.18% (subsidized) and 5.14% (unsubsidized) as of December 31, 2024 (subsidized yield reflects fee waivers and/or expense reimbursements, while unsubsidized yield does not).

Top Quarterly Contributors

Collateralized Loan Obligations (CLOs): CLO were the top contributor to performance due to strong coupon income and modest price appreciation due to tighter spreads

Asset Backed Securities: Our ABS portfolio was a positive contributor to performance due to high coupon income and price appreciation, as ABS spreads tightened throughout the year.

Top Quarterly Detractors

U.S. Treasury Bonds: U.S. Treasuries were the largest detractor to performance. With a duration of over 10 years, our Treasury portfolio experienced unrealized mark-to-market losses as interest rates rose significantly during the quarter.

Agency mortgage-backed securities (MBS): Agency MBS was the second-largest detractor to performance as interest rates rose and mortgage-backed spreads widened during the quarter.

Corporate Bonds: Corporate bonds were a slight detractor to performance as higher interest rates more than offset coupon income and modest spread tightening during the quarter.

Fund Strategy Revisited

Our approach consists primarily of investing in a diversified portfolio of high-quality bonds while maintaining an overall portfolio average duration of 3.5 to 7 years. We may seek to capture attractive coupon income and potential price appreciation by investing in longer-duration and lower-quality bonds when attractively priced. We may also invest up to 25% in fixed income securities that are not considered investment grade (such as high yield and convertible bonds as well as preferred and convertible preferred stock), and we do so when we perceive the risk/reward characteristics to be favorable.

We do not, and will not, try to mimic any particular index as we construct our portfolio. We believe our flexible mandate and high-conviction portfolio will benefit investors over the long term. We utilize a bottom-up, research-driven approach and select portfolio assets one security at a time based on our view of opportunities in the marketplace. Our fixed income research is not dependent on, but often benefits from, the due diligence work our equity teammates conduct on companies and industries.

Overall, we strive to be adequately compensated for the risks assumed to maximize investment (or reinvestment) yield and to avoid making interest rate bets, particularly those that depend on interest rates going down. We have often maintained a lower duration profile than the index, particularly in very low-yield environments. Our shorter duration profile has benefited shareholders in periods of rising interest rates.

Maintaining a liquid (e.g. Treasuries and agency MBS) and overall, up-in-quality portfolio positioning that we believe can generate higher income returns than respective indexes position us well as we begin a new year a year that could get messy in so many unpredictable ways. But also, it could be one that will particularly allow us to lean into and be liquidity providers if/when more favorable risk/reward opportunities present themselves.

We are thankful for the trust and partnership many have in our firm and its investment process. 2025: a New Year…a new blank page…. and renewed commitment to keep earning our investment partner’s trust.

data.title: Richtech Robotics: Sell On Poor Results And Elevated Valuation
data.description:
Note:

I have covered Richtech Robotics Inc. or “Richtech” (NASDAQ:RR) previously, so investors should view this as an update to my earlier article on the company.

At the beginning of the year, I initiated coverage of robotic solutions provider Richtech Robotics with a "Sell" rating due to an ugly combination of elevated business risks, persistent dilution and poor shareholder communication.

Since that time, the company has filed its annual report on form 10-K for FY2024 and its quarterly report on form 10-Q for Q1/FY2025.

As usual, Richtech did not issue an earnings press release and abstained from holding a conference call, thus leaving investors largely in the dark with regard to its financial performance.

However, taking a closer look at the company's financial results explains management's reluctance to communicate with investors on this topic:

While sales were up slightly on a year-over-year basis, revenues of just $1.3 million aren't exactly a reason to celebrate. However, the company's gross margin advanced to a new all-time high of 90.2% as the recent shift to a leasing model (Robotics-as-a-Service or "RaaS") has resulted in both revenues and related costs being recognized over the lease term.

While management has stated a number of reasons for the business model change, like creating recurring revenue streams, stronger customer relationships and reduced sales cycles, it is important to note that the Richtech is not a software company.

In fact, Richtech is trying to sell expensive robotic solutions with a focus on the hospitality and service industry.

Given the requirement for substantial upfront investments with unclear return profiles, customers have been hesitant to adopt the company's robotic solutions in recent years.

To address the issue, Richtech has not only decided to offer lease financing to potential customers, the company has also committed to operate 20 Walmart-located restaurants in Arizona, Colorado, and Texas under a franchise agreement with Ghost Kitchens America's "One Kitchen" brand.

According to the disclosures made in the 10-Q, the company has opened its first One Kitchen store in a Walmart location near Chicago last month.

One Kitchen offers a unique and interactive dining experience, showcasing the potential of our robotic technology to transform the food service industry. We anticipate opening additional One Kitchen stores near Atlanta and San Diego in late spring 2025.

Two weeks ago, Richtech also opened its first "Clouffee & Tea" store in Las Vegas:

Our first Clouffee & Tea store opened its doors in February 2025, marking our entry into the rapidly growing market for automated coffee and tea service. Clouffee & Tea combines our advanced robotics technology with a curated selection of premium beverages, creating a unique and engaging customer experience. We are confident in the potential of this brand and plan to open additional Clouffee & Tea locations in the coming months.

Last year, Richtech raised $19.4 million in net proceeds from a combined stock and warrant offering priced at $1.35 per share.

Following the meteoric rise of the company's shares over the past two months, Richtech has raised substantial, additional proceeds from warrant exercises.

However, common shareholder dilution wasn't limited to warrant exercises. During the quarter, the company issued an additional 4.1 million shares for unspecified "technology development services", 3.4 million shares to the "employee and director incentive pool" and 0.4 million shares to "advisors and consultants as compensation for services rendered".

As a result, outstanding shares have increased to 114.5 million.

Last week, Richtech agreed to issue 2.7 million new warrants with an exercise price of $4.00 to a fund managed by Armistice Capital in exchange for exercising 2.7 million existing warrants at a price of $1.35 for gross proceeds of $3.6 million.

The company also entered into a settlement agreement with AC Sunshine Securities LLC, pursuant to which Richtech agreed to pay $430,000.

Even when considering the settlement payment and assuming additional cash usage, I would estimate Richtech's current cash balance to be well above $40 million.

That said, the company's cash usage is likely to increase further due to a number of issues:

  • significantly increased investments in sales and marketing
  • requirement to provide lease financing to customers
  • material expansion of the company's One Kitchen and Clouffee & Tea store fleet

An assumed cash burn rate of $5 million per quarter would translate into approximately two years of runway for Richtech. Given this issue, I wouldn't be surprised to see the company returning to the capital markets in the not-too-distant-future.

For my part, I continue to dislike the elevated risks and substantial cash drain resulting from the recent change in the company's business model and decision to operate restaurants and coffee stores at scale.

Depending on the pace of additional store openings, Richtech's hospitality revenue will eclipse the company's core robotics business sooner rather than later, which is likely to negatively impact gross margins, particularly given the fact that Richtech has no experience in operating restaurants.

Even when generously assuming FY2025 sales to reach $10 million, the company is currently trading at a revenue multiple of more than 30x, which apparently doesn't make much sense.

In addition, with NVIDIA (NVDA) having recently disposed of its stake in Serve Robotics (SERV), the recent robotics hype might come to an end soon as momentum traders leave for assumed greener pastures:

Considering the ugly combination of elevated valuation, persistent dilution, poor investor communications and very risky business model, I am reiterating my "Sell" rating on Richtech Robotics' common shares.

Risks

A continuation of the recent robotics hype could result in momentum traders chasing the shares aggressively again.

Bottom Line

Richtech Robotics' investor communications continue to leave much to be desired, as evidenced by the fact that the company does neither issue quarterly earnings press releases nor hold conference calls.

However, a closer look at Richtech's recent SEC filings reveals a story of persistent dilution and material cash burn resulting from recent business model changes.

At this point, it is difficult to envision the company growing into its elevated valuation anytime soon if ever.

Consequently, I am reiterating my "Sell" rating on Richtech Robotics' common shares.
